text: Ladies and gentlemen, we meet today not in the quiet comfort of abstract theory.
We meet while the smoke still curls from the craters in Kashmir,
and while the echoes of the sirens from this past May still ring in the A's of South Asia.
The opposition will tell you that new Delhi is a rational actor,
and a dangerous neighborhood.
I'll tell you that is a lie.
The opposition will try to sell you a story of security dilemmas
and deterrence to not buy it.
India's policy towards Pakistan is no longer about the line of control.
It is about the line of credit.
The political credit that neither Modi needs to withdraw from the bank of public sphere
every single time his domestic popularity crashes.
Now before I dismantle the machinery up behind their lies,
it is my great pleasure to introduce my great friends inside opposition.
Speaking first is a very good friend of mine.
I'm my chief of staff, Varang Pondushali.
Varang like as also an Indian, my first chief of staff was also an Indian,
but my first chief of staff lasted only a little bit longer than their file jets in a narrow space.
You would have thought that I had learned my lesson from that,
but no, I picked my second chief of staff to be an Indian as well.
But after working with both of them,
I can personally say I have lived through India's entire Pakistan policy,
because Varang is exactly like that.
Loud, high and emotion, and when everything goes wrong,
instead of introspecting and reflecting that like the Indians,
resorts to only one tactic, blame the Pakistani president.
His girlfriend breaks up with him.
What is Varang's new?
Blame the Pakistani president.
He gets punched in the face by a mobster.
What is Varang's new?
Blame the Pakistani president.
And if he loses his election tomorrow,
I'm telling you, I trust me, he will blame the Pakistani president.
Speaking next is the Vartron Bannerjee, a defail in the business school,
another very good friend of mine.
Now the Vartron is running in two oxygen elections in the past.
And sadly he lost both of them.
Now after those thinking defeats,
he has decided to take a page out of the BJP's election playbook.
Which is what?
Stan and Chamber blame the Pakistani's everything,
and you too can win election.
You otherwise would have lost the Vartron.
Speaking third is Sid Nagarath.
Now Sid too, an Indian server chief of staff,
to work previous ex-Pakistanni president.
Now if Pakistan really represents such a security challenge,
then after so many Pakistani presidents working so well with the Indian chief of staff,
they would have heard some reports of some terrorism happening again.
But let me be clear in my speech.
My target is not the Indian citizens.
Who themselves are a great victim to this lie?
When my target is the architect of this lie,
I stand here to expose the regime of the Render Modi,
a government that has turned the world's largest democracy into the world's largest state for hate.
How one man's reckless electoral pandering is endangering the lies and the hopes of billions of people.
This is not a security policy.
It is a popular strategy written in South front ink and sealed with a blood of soldiers,
designed for one purpose and one purpose only to keep a regime in power by manufacturing a permanent enemy.
Let us look at the facts, not the propaganda, but the bloodstained facts.
On the 7th of May 2025, the Indian Air Force launched operations in Doudu.
They called it a surgical strike.
The Indian media cheered, they showed you graphics of fighter jets, destroying terror camps.
But let me tell you what they didn't know show you.
Let me take you to city of Baháverkorn.
Let me tell you about the mosque that was not a training camp, but a place of worship.
Whose domes were shattered by Indian missiles, while people melted prayer.
Pakistani authorities reported that 31 innocent civilians were killed.
15 of them were children.
15 children torn from their mothers, not by accident, but by a calculated act of aggression.
While Mr. Modi's news anchors were celebrating precision, Pakistani fathers were digging through concrete.
That is the cost of security policy.
And for what? When the tragedy? And it is a severe tragedy what happened in Balagam?
On the 23rd of April, after that tragedy, our prime minister Shabashri stood before the world and condemned what happened in Balagam.
He did not ask for war, he asked for truth.
He offered a joint independent international inquiry. He opened the door to investigators.
And what did Mr. Modi do? Did he pick up the phone? Did he send a diplomat?
Did he provide a single shred of credible evidence to the United Nations?
No. He sent the Air Force.
Independent observers and the multiple media sources reported that India struck before any evidence was verified.
And why is that the case? Well, because the joint inquiry takes time.
And Mr. Modi did not have time. He had an election to win.
Mr. Speaker, I will set out the anatomy of their spectacle.
What is the step one? A tragedy happens.
Step two, before the bodies even get cold, the media frenzy begins.
Not asking who did this, but screaming revenge.
And step three, the missiles fly. They call this security.
But let's just call it for what it is. It is populism and uniform.
And it's work, didn't it? Look at the headlines from this week.
In November 2025, the BJP swept the elections in Bihar.
The same commentators who said Modi was finished, are not calling him the protector.
He sold them fair and that bought him a landslide.
That is the policy of transaction. It is not a policy of defense.
It is not a policy of distraction.
But Mr. Speaker, to call this mere election hearing is to be dangerously naive.
This is not just about one election. It is about a soul.
The soul of the current Indian regime is fascist.
We must speak the truth that Proveller diplomacy tries to hide.
Prime Minister Modi is not a low-neckeder. He is the obedient soldier of RSS.
You cannot understand Modi with understanding the RSS.
And you cannot understand the RSS with understanding the sentence that their ideologue,
Golvarka wrote about Nazi Germany. He called Nazi Germany a good lesson for us in Hindustan to learn and profit by.
That is the ideology that sits in the Prime Minister's office today.
That is the legacy his leadership lies on.
From the ashes of the Gujarat riots in 2002, where Mr. Modi presided over the slaughter of a thousand Muslims to the destruction of the Babri Musjid,
their playbook has remained consistent.
Power is consolidated by manufacturing, a permanent enemy,
their version of a Hindu raster, a pure ethnoviligest state cannot exist without another.
They need Pakistan.
You have to stand up, you want to point permission.
They need Pakistan. If Pakistan disappeared tomorrow, the BJP would have to invent us.
Because without the monster next to all, they would have to answer to their own people for the hunger, the poverty and the economic turbulence.
So let me tell you again, they do not fight because we are threat to their borders.
They fight us because we are necessary for their ballots.
Mr. Speaker, the opposition will tell you that these military actions are driven by intelligence.
I tell you they are driven by the election commission of India's calendar.
Let's look at the timeline. It's not a conspiracy.
It's a clockwork pattern that insults the intelligence of this house.
Picture the scene in late 2016.
The BJP is fighting a losing battle for the heart of Othapur desh.
The polls were clear, the outcome of the grim.
So what did Modi do?
He turned a terrorist attack, a tragedy in order into a political spectacle,
and just like that a looming fight, a looming defeat was transformed into a political landscape victory.
Foss forward to 2019, the general election is looming, and the government's economic report card is weak, but just like before, the narrative flips.
Suddenly we have Palwama, we have Balakot, the prime minister's platform, Pivots, instantly from creating jobs to teaching Pakistan a lesson and defending India.
And most of the result, history repeats itself, another landslavic tree.
And now in 2015 in 2025 April, the BJP was staring at a loss in the Bihara elections.
They already suffered below expectation results in the 2024 looks of elections.
And the people were asking questions that the government could not answer.
Because what happened? Like a magician pulling a rabbit from a hat, we got operations in Duran May.
Mr. Modi lies to his people. He mocks their intelligence at innocent Pakistani lies by the price.
Mr. Modi does not address domestic unemployment. He does not address domestic inequality. He does not address the economics low down.
And why? Because he is in the people of doing so. He lies to the Indian people, the Pakistan is the threat. He lies to them, the Pakistan is the obstacle to their progress.
He lies to them, the Pakistan is a danger waiting across the border. Denied, sit down.
But members, these are not security assessments. They are political calculations. Every time an election nearers, the drones of war grow louder.
Every time his approval dips, his rhetoric of fear rises. Every time accountability approaches the cross border strikes mysteriously reappear.
And what's the greatest tragedy of it all? Is that his strategy undermines the very security that it claims to protect innocent civilians die?
So his premiership can live another day. And if that were not enough, they have now crossed a line that civilization itself hold hell sacred.
Mr. Speaker, I'm talking about the Indus water treaty. On April 23rd, 2025 India announced the suspension of this treaty, a pact that had survived three wars, but they did not stop in announcements.
Using water, it's a basic human right as a weapon of war is not just despicable, but it's an act of hydrotarism.
They are threatening to parse the throats of our children. They are threatening to turn the breadboss kit of Punjab into a dust bowl.
Since when did a security policy involve starving a neighbor? Since when did the right to thirst become a tool of diplomatic leverage?
By holding a vital resource, a vital shared resource hostage, Delhi has signaled to the world that it is willing to weaponize nature itself. This is not the behavior of a regional superpower.
It is the behavior of a neighborhood bully who thinks respect can be demanded at the top of a dam.

Mr. Speaker, members of the house, when water becomes a bargaining chip and not a human right, you know this is not about security. It is about cruelty and cruelty is the last refuge of a populist highland.
Mr. Speaker, we must lift our eyes from the border to the horizon. If you truly want to understand the architecture of Mr. Modi's India, do not look to Gandhi spinning wheel.
But look to Benjamin Netanyahu's war cabinet. What we are witnessing is the is realization of Indian statecraft.
Mr. Modi and Mr. Netanyahu are ideological twins. They are brothers in hate.
Netanyahu, survived by invoking the eternal threat of the Arabs and Modi is surviving by invoking the eternal threat of the Muslims.
Both represent a dangerous shift from civic nationalism to ethno religious nationalism.
Zionism claims that the land is for the Jews alone. Hinduism claims that the land for the Hindus alone. Both leaders use this permanent politics of siege, Berger.
And both leaders use this permanent politics of siege to dismantle their own Jewishries, control their own press and escape their own corruption scandals.
And where do we see the darkest laboratory for this? It's in Kashmir.
The opposition will call Kashmir an internal matter for the Indians. I call it a settler-colonial project.

Today, Kashmir is the most militarized zone in the world. There is one soldier for every 10 Kashmiris. Think about that ratio. That is not about protection. That is not about security. That is a prison.
Mr. Modi abrogated Article 370, not to integrate the region but to conquer it.
They are importing settlers from the mainland to change the demographics to turn a Muslim majority into a Muslim minority.
In their own ancestral home, this is not security policy. It is demographic engineering sold as integration.
Members of the House, Mr. Speaker, I conclude with this.
The opposition might ask, because India not have the legitimate security concerns, of course they do. So does Pakistan. So does every nation in this fractured world.
But there is a difference. It is a difference between a surgeon who cuts to heal and a butcher who cuts to Salah's meat.
And Mr. Modi has never lifted a scalper for his country's security only a butcher's knife to secure his own elections.
This policy, the cycle of strikes, suspension of treaties, manufactured hysteria, is not a cure is a poison.

And here is the tragedy. Here is the real tragedy wall of this. The ultimate victim of this poison is not Pakistan. We are resilient. We have survived wars. We have survived floods. We have survived the loss of our children. We have survived Mr. Modi's ego.
To the Indian students in the Chamber, my friends, we are your partners in the future that we really have together.
One of the biggest victims of terrorism is Pakistan itself. We have lost over 80,000 of our own men, women and children, 150 billion dollars of economic damage down to the country.
Why would anyone burn their own house down to attack their neighbor? Why would we do this?
We speak the same language. We share the same cinema and the same music. My argument today is against those who are hijacking the soul of your country.
To build their power on fear. Being sold alive, you will be told that your safety requires the hunger of your neighbor.
You will be told that to be a patriot. You must hate those with the same language, same culture and same customs.
But let me remind you, the fire that your government is lighting on the border will not just stay on the border. It will come home.
And that fire will burn your press freedom. It will burn your independent courts. And it will burn the secular soul that once made India a light in the world.
This security policy is a fraud. It is a populism. It is populism that risks nuclear winter just to secure a political summer.
I asked this house. I asked this house. Do not applaud the arsonist just because he wears a uniform. Do not validate a strategy that trades the blood of soldiers for the ballot of politicians.
Vote for the proposition. Vote to tell the truth that the politics of fear is a game we are no longer willing to play.
Vote that yes. India's policy to Pakistan is a populist strategy sold in a lie as a security policy.
Thank you very much everyone. Thank you. Thank you for that speech.
Thank you for the President. I thank myself for my speech.
I now look to my great chief of staff. We are on the board of the K-thro side opposition.
Mr. President, on the members of the union. Our distinguished guests on the proposition bench. Thank you for the flow.
I rise today. The day after the solemn anniversary that haunts everyone by car.
To oppose the motion that India's policy towards Pakistan is populism disguised as security policy.
In plain terms, I stand here to tell you that not only is this claim false but it is a fundamental mistreating of my country's reality.
And I do so not just as a member of the Oxford Union but as a Mumbaier but as someone who has lived through the consequences of the Indian of the India Pakistan saga in his own home and heart.
Yesterday was 2611 November 26th which in my city is not an ordinary date on the calendar.
It marks the day when in 2008, 10 terrorists stormed across iconic sites across my hometown, unleashing three days of bloodshed.
One of those targets was chaturperthi shivaji Maharaj terminus, the very station that my aunt passed through almost every evening.
By chance, or by Providence, she took a different train home that night.

Not only escaping the fate of the 166 souls that did not. Another target was the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel where my best friends father, a major in the National Security Guard, was one of the first commandos to repel into a burning inferred up.
I was a school boy that glued to the television as my city burned. I remember the fear in my mother's voice on the phone, the tension in my father's clenched jaw.
For three nights, Mumbai did not sleep and neither did I. As a child, I learnt too early that security is not an abstract policy issue.
It is the difference between life and death. The difference between a phone call from a loved one or a silence that lasts forever.
I share this, not to darken the mood, but to ground this debate in reallishing. For me, this isn't just an intellectual exercise. It's painfully personal.
The rehabilitation of 200 meters from my childhood home was bombed in the 1993 serial blast that killed over 250 people.

I grew up under the shadows of these tragedies. My parents were here on the Trimber Tonight listening angstlessly to their son raised me with the stories of those dark bits and the manker that we must always be alert.
When someone claims that India's staff stands towards Pakistan is really populism, masquerading as security policy, you might understand why I bristle.
It sounds like someone telling you that the locks on your nose are just for sure not because there are robberies in the neighborhood.
But before we delve deeper into that notion, let me first address the three gentlemen, a raid against me.
A rather formidable proposition bench if there ever was one. They say this house loves a good show. My opponents have certainly delivered on the casting.
First, we have our union president, Mr. Musa Haraj, leading the charge for the proposition.
Musa is not only the president of this union, but also Pakistani by birth and a very, very dear friend of mine.
In fact, as the Chief of Staff, I've spent months working with him, which makes tonight a tad bit awkward, because technically I'm supposed to make him look good.
But here I am, last night writing his speech for him.
Musa, you've had an interesting term, haven't you? Just a few weeks ago.
One information? Yes.
So you're admitting in a chamber that what I just said was written by an Indian you.
To respond, I should gladly admit that sometimes it takes an Indian to clean up the incompetence of a Pakistani.
Now, Musa, you've had an interesting term, haven't you?
Just a few weeks ago, Musa survived a historic no-confidence vote right here in the Oxford Union.
For the uninitiated, yes, Oxford Union presidents sometimes face more rebellions than actual heads of state.
The world failed and Musa sailed through unscathed with a thumping majority.
Making him argue, the only Pakistani-origin leader in history to face a no-confidence vote and remain in power the next morning.
I tease my friend of course, but there's a serious point. Musa's victory was a triumph of transparent, open, and democratic processes.
And Oxford Union specialty?
Now, now, now, now, only if some folks in Ralbindi could say the same.
And look who sits with you, the glorious ghost of the presidency's past.
We have Ahmad Nawaz, the president for Michael Mastur, 2022.
Ahmad, your man of resilience, he survived the horrors of the APS attack.
You have my utmost respect for that for the rest of my life.
But you also survived barely so.
The internal politics of this chamber, you hold the unique distinction of being the only Pakistani-president in history who was not removed by a coup, but an auto-resignation.
An auto-resignation fall, missing access committee meetings.
Now, the official record says that he will busy.
We all saw the Instagram stories of you being shot with bad baby.
Now, it doesn't Oxford Union president have to be caught outside.
Following on, we have Mr. Hanukkah.
The president of Henry Term 25.
Mr. Arth, this is a special moment for me.
In my first term here, you were Ahmad's chief of stuff.
You were the man behind the throne.
You watched him fall, you fought the dirty politics, and like a true survivor, you climbed the ladder to become president yourself.
And the chief of staff now.
I have seen the work that you walked, I have taken notes.
You taught me that the Oxford Union, like in Islamabad, chaos isn't a pit.
Chaos is a ladder.
So when I look at you over there, arguing against India having mastered British institutional survivalism,
I don't know whether to debuts you or ask you for a reference letter.
Now, we're all acquainted with the spirit of the Oxford Union debate.
A bit of bite, a bit of banter.
Let's get to the meat of the matter.
The motion asserts that India's policy towards Pakistan is populism disguised as security policy.
In other words, that India's cynically fashions its Pakistan strategy just to win both rewards rather than genuinely protected itself.
As the first speaker of the opposition, I reject this whole heart and me.
I will show you step by step, how India's policy is primarily driven by legitimate security concerns and not cheap populism.
And in doing so, I should also flip the mirror to our fence because if any nation has suffered from the leaders who wrap themselves in the flag of popularity, it isn't India, it is a Pakistan.
The proposition is argument. It's a doctor. It suggests that the Indian states manufacture its threats.
We invent Pokemon that we use the border as a ballot box to whip our ports.
They argue that when India strikes back, we're not protecting our citizens. We're performing for them.
To win this debate, I do not need to use rhetoric. I simply need to use a calendar.
If the proposition is true, if the Indian security policy is just populism, then crises should corroborate with the elections, the bombs should go off when the polls open.
Let's look at the data and let us be inductive.
I grew up in the heart of Mumbai. My home was not too far from where in March 1993, RDX explosives ripped through Plaza Cinema.
It was a part of a coordinated series of 12 blasts that turned my city into a mock.
257 people died. I was not born there. But I grew up with the creator in left and outside key. Now ask yourself, was there an election in March 1993?
No. That election was three years away. Was the government looking for a populist distraction?
No. The government was weak. The economy was opening up and we were desperate for stability.
Terror did not come because we needed a vote. It came because Dow was in the ISI wanted to fracture India's financial smile.
That was not populism. That was an act of war. I run my aunt once again, vividly.
Driving through the Charter of the Shrivalji Terminus. She put out of the train station on 927 pm and the terrorist entered at 930.
Three minutes is the difference between my aunt watching from home tonight and her being engraved in a memorial stone in South Bombay.

Ten minutes is the city of 20 million hostage. Now look at the policy response. The proposition says that India's populist, what would a populist government do after 2611, the public raid was nuclear.
I remember it. We wanted blood. We wanted the Air Force to flatten the training camps in Mudurke. A populist leader would have just launched the jets to win the next election.
But the government of the day, the Congress party chose strategic restraint. They chose diplomacy. They chose to send dossiers.
They appealed to the international community. They appealed to you. Did it work? Did it bias? Did the non-popular approach bias piece? No. It borders but Hankot. It borders Uri. It borders Bulwama.
We learned a hard lesson in Mr. President. You cannot shame a state that has no shame.
Let's move to the present. Six months ago, the proposition claims this commission is this may were election hearing.
Check the dates. The elections for the Indian-General Parliament were over. The government was secure.
On 22nd of April, terrorists from the Resistance Front, a cheap rebrand of the Lashkarayah tribe attacked tourists in Belgang. They dragged out 26 people and killed them for their religion.
They didn't ask for their vote for. They executed them. India's response, Operation Sendur was launched on 7th May. Not the name. Sendur. The Vimilian mark of a Hindu wife.
It was not Operation Thunder or Operation Rath. It was named for the Vimilian. It was a surgical dismantling of nine launch bats.
We degraded trade-offs in Lahore. We punished the perpetrators and then what we stopped.
We did not invade. We did not occupy. We did not hold the parade in Delhi. We re-established deterrent. We re-established deterrents and went back to work.
That is not populism. That is professionalism. The proposition confuses popular with populist. Yes, Mr. President, defending your citizens from being murdered is popular.
Providing clean water is popular. Keeping the railway safe is popular. That does not make it a trick.
The populism is promising easy answers to complex problems. Security is the hard, expensive dangerous work of ensuring that a child and the other can go to the cinema without being blown up.
But, if you want to see, the actual populism describes a security. You must only look across the Rath cliff line.

When India launched Operation Sendur, how did Pakistan respond? Did it fix its security gaps? No. Operation Binyanal Masuz was launched. The solid cemented wall, a chronic reference, designed not for tactical efficacy.
A religious mobilization, launched missiles, and a member had the shot down, had 11 bases struck and achieved a best tactical statement.
After all of that, what do you do? You release a music video. The ISPR, your military's PR wing, released an anthem, darkened by A.K. Nam Pakistan.
Mr. President, when India fights a war, we debrief the pilots. In Pakistan, they ought to join the chorus.
You held victory rallies on the 16th of May. You declared a national holiday, why? Because you cannot give your people bread, so you must give them the circus.

You cannot fix the price of flowers, so you fix it on the Indian threat. You need the enemy of the gate without the Indian monster. Your own people may turn around and ask, why general's own Papa John's franchises, while the country defaults on its loans?
That is populism. That is the alchemy of turning public poverty into private power with the specter of all.

When you vote for this motion, you are telling the videos of Bellgarm that their grief is just a prop for the government. You are telling my parents sitting right there when they're at the fear that they failed when the windows shook in 93.
Or the panic they failed when my aunt didn't answer the phone in 2008 was just a disguise. It is easy to deconstruct security policy in the Oxford Union.
Our walls at the thick windows are well plastered. The world feels safe. We can debate narratives and discourse over port and trees.
But out there in the real world, the wolf is at the door. India has decided to stop feeding the wolf. We have decided to stop sending a dossier. We have decided to shoot it.
That is not populism. That is the biological imperative of a nation that refuses to die. We do not want war. We want to be boring neighbors. We want to trade onions and electricity.
We want to play your cricket team without worrying if the away team is bringing bats to see fall.
But until the state that defense itself stops using terror as an instrument to foreign policy we will keep our power to dry. We will keep our radars on and we will keep our guards up.
If that is populism then I am a populist. If that is a disguise then I will wear it because the alternative is not liberalism. The alternative is 2611 on repeat.
Tonight I may have been belligerent in defending my country stance. But I also hope that I have been fair and fair.
I do not hate Pakistan. I truly wish to see Pakistan that is safe, stable, prosperous and at peace with us.
I dream of a day where I could take a train from Mumbai to Lahore and have Kababs in the Bahamundi.
When the Indian cricket team captain and the Pakistani cricket team captain can talk about their schools, their kids schools.
Because politics is a cease to matter. I am an idealist by heart. But unfortunately I am a realist by circumstance.
But for that day to come you must first deal in truth. The truth is calling India's Pakistan policy populism in disguise is a dangerous decision.
It implies that we are all just reacting that there is no wolf on the door but there is a recognising this wolf.
It doesn't mean we should keep living in fear of it. It just means that we need to know that to have the bigger stick when you are trying to build the bigger fence.
Perhaps in a non-sodistant future a young divider from Mumbai and a young divider from Lahore, Keta or Pechawar.
We will stand here not to argue about war and policy but to argue about who has the better music, or who has the better peyani, obviously the answer to both is in danger.
Jokes aside, that is the debate I want to have. We are on day it to get there.
We must first ensure that the policy is rooted in reality and security and not lost in the fog of propaganda.
India is doing its part by treating security with the gravity of deserves populism. We leave to the TV studios. Ladies and gentlemen, I beg you to oppose the motion. Thank you.
Thank you very much. You still have a job so it didn't go that bad.
I now look to the ex-president and regions, poor college to continue the case for side proposition.
Thank you Mr. President, members of the House, ladies and gentlemen.
When the government tells you that this is about security, you should always ask a second question.
Security for whom and against what?
This motion has two parts to it. First India's policy and then the policy we have is a security.
Tonight on this side of the House we already heard from the president to continue the case for side proposition.
I'll focus on this specific element. What policy are we talking about?
In order to understand that policy, it is very important to look at in the domestic context.
It's once you understand the domestic policy, then can you only understand the external attitude of India towards Pakistan.
And when we speak on this side of the House on this motion, when you talk about India's policy, we are talking about India's policies as we speak now in India's policy under Modi.
And on this side of the House it is our position that this policy is a populist policy.
A populist, a populist strategy needs enemies of the people.
And in the India's domestic context under Narendra Modi, that enemy is drawn from the Muslim population and from minorities and abroad, it is Pakistan.
India, tonight I will show you, factually historically and morally, that this policy is not a security doctrine.
It is a Hindu-Twar populism that does not align with the homogeneous nation that the India is.
Or with the secularism or the constitution of the very state that Modi thinks it stands for.
However, it is important before I do that to respond to some points from my friends from side or position.
We will already heard from Iran, I will continue to hear from Iran, that Pakistan propagates terrorism.
The Pakistan is a security threat because it poses dangerous to the population of India.
Let's be clear that Pakistan is the largest victim of terrorism in the South Asia.
It has suffered since the war of terror started in 2001, 50,000 people lost to the war on terror.
In this very house, we want to talk about the terrorist attacks in India.
We do not deny that there have been terrorist attacks in India and our heart goes to the Indian people and we are in India on that.
But it is Pakistan, that is the victim of terrorism.
In this very house we have people sitting who have been the survivors of the terrorist attacks that are times have been sponsored by India proxies in Pakistan.
It has lost 126 billion since 2001 just to the war on terror.
So we as a nation have suffered the most when we talk about terrorism.
And part of that is also contributed to the proxy wars in India's operating in Pakistan.
In my state, Balochistan, in the form of supporting the Balochistan liberation army, today I cannot walk out after 5pm in my own province.
Because of the proxy elements of India, they do not allow for free-flow Pakistanis to go there or Balochistanis to be there.
The Tariqah Taliban Pakistan, that operates in the tribal areas of Pakistan, that is the largest threat to the sovereignty of Pakistan.
The sponsored again by Indian proxies.
So when we talk about terrorism, Pakistan is the victim and yes India has suffered.
But to contribute there to Pakistan is absurdity.
Take the example of the recent program attack.
Pakistan was very open to the world to come and investigate to put together an international inquiry as to who is responsible for these attacks.
Instead of participating in that inquiry, what does Modi do?
Because it does not suit him to have inquiries because he will show that Pakistan is not in war.
Instead, he acts in a war of aggression, attacking civilians in Pakistan across the board.
Now that is Modi's policy.
A policy where he gets a moment, where he gets a moment to unite people, the Hindu population and majority against Pakistan, he will take advantage of that.
Can a head of state in Pakistan be non-Muslim?
It is extremely irrelevant whether a head of state in Pakistan came in.
The prime minister of Pakistan is all the powers and the prime minister of Pakistan can be Muslim.
I hope that answers your question.
So let's talk about India's policies internally.
Let's talk, let's look into it in Modi's own words.
Human rights watch analyzed in 2024 alone, 173 speeches of Modi before the election.
And a hundred and ten of those speeches, they were about Islamophobia, the war against minorities in India, all as a pattern to unite the Hindu population.
The common population to create fear, to create a common enemy, so he can utilize that for his own benefits.
A prime minister who has been summoned prime minister twice before runs for the third time, and his speeches says the following.
The Muslims are infiltrators, they've threatened the safety of our children and daughter.
He says that the opposition will bulldoze the Ram temple and return Hindu gods to attend creating fear.
Fear that the largest minority in India is the thing to worry about after this I will come to you.
He goes on, the Muslims are trying to destroy our gods.

Mr. President, I must clarify, I stand here not just as a Pakistani, but as someone who believes in what this chamber stands for.

Not as a conflict zone, as they were telling you, but as a corridor of trade, energy and connectivity. Secondly, global leaders and American administrations and leaders around the world have publicly praised Pakistan over the last few weeks for its role in regional security.

I lost my younger brother in that attack, and I lost some of my closest friends.
And from that moment, it wasn't a sentiment against India or a sentiment against Afghanistan or a sentiment against a particular group.
